coredata.dc:title: Engineering hybrid artificial interfacial layer with Zn-ion channels for stable zinc anodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Xi
Wang, Jingjing
Xu, Jing
Li, Hongjun
Zhang, Hongwei
Zuo, Danying
coredata.dc:description: As a promising anode material for aqueous zinc-ion energy storage devices, zinc metal is garnering significant attention. However, it suffers from dendritic growth and side reactions. To mitigate the above issues, a strategy of constructing a hybrid artificial interfacial layer with Zn-ion channels on Zn anode surfaces is successfully developed. The protective coating on Zn foil forms a hydrophilic/hydrophobic phase separation structure. The hydrophobic phase prevents direct contact between the electrolyte and the Zn anode, mitigating water-related side reactions. The hydrophilic phase not only regulates the Zn2+ distribution, but also promotes Zn2+ migration, leading to uniform stripping/plating. Consequently, the symmetric cells with coated Zn electrodes deliver an outstanding cycling performance of 800 h. Remarkably, the assembled zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitor achieves a stable cycling performance of up to 20,000 cycles. This study sheds light on Zn anode protection for practical Zn-metal-based energy storage systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc anode
Zn dendrite
Side reaction
Artificial interfacial layer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction, morphology, and properties of the SPEEK/CD layer
Suppressed side reactions and zinc dendrite of Zn@SPEEK/30%CD
Electrochemical performance of ZIHS devices with coated Zn anode

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the open fund of the Hubei Engineering Research Center for Industrial Detonator Intelligent Assembly of Wuhan Textile University (WGK202402001).

We acknowledge the Analytical and Testing Center of Wuhan Textile University for SEM testing. This work was supported by the open fund of the Hubei Engineering Research Center for Industrial Detonator Intelligent Assembly of Wuhan Textile University (WGK202402001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The structure formula of SPEEK with a hydrophobic aromatic backbone and pendant hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups. (b) Schematic structure of CDs. (c) Schematic illustration of the mechanism of Zn dendrite growth and water-induced side reaction for a bare Zn anode. (d) Schematic illustration of the mechanism of the as-prepared SPEEK/CD layer for suppressing Zn dendrite growth and water-induced side reaction.

(a) The optical image of the CD solution under UV light of 365 nm. (b) PL excitation spectra of the CD excited by UV light of 365 nm. (c) FTIR spectra of the CD. (d) TEM image of the CD. (e) The cross-sectional SEM image of Zn@SPEEK/30%CD. Top-view SEM images of (f) bare Zn foil, and (g) Zn@SPEEK/30 % CD contacted with 2 M ZnSO4 for 15 days. (h) The contact angles of bare Zn and Zn@SPEEK/30 % CD.

Bare Zn and Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes: (a) LSV curves. (b) Tafel curves. (c) The voltage-time curves of symmetric cells. (d) CV curves of Zn plating/stripping in asymmetric cells. (e) Zn2+ transference number. (f) The activation energy of Zn electrodeposition in symmetric cells.

Bare Zn and Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes: (a) Rate performance of symmetric cells. (b) Cycling performance of symmetric cells at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2, (c) Corresponding voltage-capacity curves at the selected cycles of symmetric cells. (d) Zn reversibility in asymmetric cells. (e) The optical microscopy images of Zn deposition morphology on bare Zn and Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes. Top-view SEM images of (f) Bare Zn and (g) Zn@SPEEK/30%CD after 50 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 in symmetric cells.

The ZIHS with Zn@SPEEK/30%CD anode: (a) CV curves under different voltage windows, (b) CV curves under various scanning rates, (c) GCD curves under different current densities, (d) Rate performance from 0.5 to 5 A g−1, (e) Nyquist plot (Inset shows the equivalent circuit model), (f) Cycling performance at 5 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Engineering hybrid artificial interfacial layer with Zn-ion channels for stable zinc anodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Engineering Research Center for Industrial Detonator Intelligent Assembly

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Engineering Research Center for Industrial Detonator Intelligent Assembly, Wuhan Textile University, WuHan, 430073, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, WuHan, 430073, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a promising anode material for aqueous zinc-ion energy storage devices, zinc metal is garnering significant attention. However, it suffers from dendritic growth and side reactions. To mitigate the above issues, a strategy of constructing a hybrid artificial interfacial layer with Zn-ion channels on Zn anode surfaces is successfully developed. The protective coating on Zn foil forms a hydrophilic/hydrophobic phase separation structure. The hydrophobic phase prevents direct contact between the electrolyte and the Zn anode, mitigating water-related side reactions. The hydrophilic phase not only regulates the Zn2+ distribution, but also promotes Zn2+ migration, leading to uniform stripping/plating. Consequently, the symmetric cells with coated Zn electrodes deliver an outstanding cycling performance of 800 h. Remarkably, the assembled zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitor achieves a stable cycling performance of up to 20,000 cycles. This study sheds light on Zn anode protection for practical Zn-metal-based energy storage systems.

Zinc anode protective layers composed of sulfonated polyether ether ketone and carbon dots not only inhibit the growth of zinc dendrites, but also supress water-related side reactions.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the global emphasis on renewable energy increases, energy forms such as wind and solar, which are characterized by significant fluctuations, urgently require effective energy storage technologies to balance supply and demand on the power grid. Aqueous zinc-ion energy storage devices, e.g. zinc-ion batteries (ZIB) and zinc-ion hybrid supercapacitors (ZIHS), play a significant role in the balance and dispatch of renewable energy due to their advantages such as high theoretical capacity, low cost, low safety risks, eco-friendliness, and large abundance [1,2]. Consequently, they have considerable potential of to address the instability inherent in renewable energy generation. Meanwhile, aqueous zinc anodes still face critical challenges, including high thermodynamic instability and undesirable parasitic reactions, which severely hinder the practical application of aqueous zinc-ion energy storage devices [2]. The inherent thermodynamic instability frequently induces the formation of zinc dendrites, which not only compromise device safety through separator penetration and potential short circuits but also degrade electrode integrity, ultimately leading to active material loss and diminished cycling performance. Concurrently, parasitic reactions manifest primarily through two mechanisms: the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and corrosion processes. The HER induces electrolyte depletion and hydrogen gas evolution, resulting in device swelling and capacity fade, while corrosion reactions consume active zinc material and generate insulating byproducts, thereby increasing internal resistance and reducing energy conversion efficiency. These interrelated phenomena synergistically accelerate performance deterioration, significantly curtailing the operational lifespan of zinc-ion devices [3].

To address the aforementioned issues, a couple of improvement strategies have been developed, which can be primarily categorized into three aspects: (1) Anode design, including in-built solid electrolyte interphase engineering [4], artificial interfacial layer engineering [5–23], crystallographic engineering [24,25], and zinc host engineering [26]; (2) Electrolyte optimization, such as adopting water-in-salt electrolytes [27,28], adding functional additives [29–41], and introducing organic solvents [42–45]; (3) Separator modification or gel polymer electrolyte exploration [46–53]. Among them, artificial interfacial layer engineering is an effective approach to prohibit dendrite growth and suppress hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) corrosion. Various artificial interfacial layers have been constructed to enhance the interfacial stability of Zinc anodes, including inorganic coating layers (e.g. NaTi2(PO4)3 [6], metal-organic framework [7], covalent organic framework [13], vermiculite [9], ZnF2 [54], TiO2 [55], CaCO3 [56], ZnO [57], montmorillonite [58], and graphene [59]), organic coating layers (e.g. SPEEK [8,60,61], semi-interpenetrating network [14], lignin [15], and polyvinyl alcohol [17]), and organic-inorganic hybrid coating layers [5,62]. Generally, the inorganic coating layers often have poor adhesion to zinc electrodes and are prone to cracking or peeling from zinc electrodes due to large volume changes of zinc electrodes during long-term charging and discharging processes. While the organic coating layers usually have high interfacial transport impedance and slow Zn2+ migration due to narrow ion transfer channels. The organic-inorganic hybrid coating layers combine the advantages of both the inorganic coating layer and the organic coating layers, which can transform the diffusion transport mechanism of Zn2+ ions into the fast jump transport mechanism along the interface layer [62]. However, the reported organic-inorganic hybrid coating layers with Nafion as the polymer matrix are not favorable for large-scale application owing to the high cost of Nafioin.

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanosized carbon materials with fluorescence property, which consists of the intrinsic inner structure of sp2/sp3 carbon and hydrophilic surface chemical groups [63]. Sulfonated polyether ether ketone (SPEEK) is an aromatic polymer with pendant sulfonic acid groups, which is not conducive to the formation of broad hydrophilic channel structures [64]. After CDs are incorporated into SPEEK to form an organic-inorganic hybrid coating layer, the hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups on SPEEK are easy to assemble around hydrophilic CDs and construct a hydrophilic/hydrophobic phase separation structure with broad hydrophilic channels for zinc ions (Fig. 1 ). The hydrophobic regions in the artificial SPEEK/CD interfacial layer can prevent the zn anode from contacting the electrolyte and improve the corrosion impedance. Simultaneously, the chemical groups in hydrophilic regions can not only shield anions and free H2O molecules, but also promote desolvation of hydrated zinc ions and the kinetics of Zn plating/stripping. As a result, the protected Zn symmetric cell demonstrates a long cycle life of over 800 h at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2. Furthermore, the ZIHS with the coated Zn anode and activated carbon (AC) exhibits superior cycling stability with ⁓90 % capacitance retention and a coulombic efficiency (CE) above 99 % after 20,000 charge/discharge cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction, morphology, and properties of the SPEEK/CD layer
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows that the solution of CDs emits a bright blue color under UV excitation at 365 nm, which is consistent with the peak value (443 nm) of the photoluminescence (PL) excitation spectra under the UV light of 365 nm in Fig. 2b. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CDs is presented in Fig. 2c. The main absorption bands are respectively assigned to N-H and O-H stretching vibrations at 3338 cm−1, C-H stretching vibrations at 2902 cm−1, -C=O vibration at 1652 and 1560 cm−1, and C-O vibration at 1030 cm−1, revealing hydroxyl groups, carboxylic groups and amine groups on the surface of CDs [65]. From the transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of CDs (Fig. 2d and Fig. S1a), it can be observed that CDs have a diameter of about 3–5 nm. Furthermore, CDs marked with yellow circles in Fig. 2d exhibit high crystallinity with a lattice parameter of about 0.31 nm, which is close to the interlayer distance between the graphene layers (002 facet) [66]. It suggests that CDs are favorable for the construction of the nanoscale hydrophilic phase (or channels) with sulfonic acid groups on the SPEEK chains in the artificial SPEEK/CD interfacial layer due to the abundant hydrophilic groups.

The CDs are incorporated into SPEEK solutions for forming artificial interfacial layers on Zn foils (Fig. S2). The four ZIHS devices are assembled by AC cathodes and coated Zn anodes to evaluate electrochemical performances. As shown in Fig. S3, the ZIHS device with the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD delivers the largest cyclic voltammetry (CV) area and the longest discharging time. Therefore, the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD is selected for further study. Compared to the bare Zn (Fig. S1b), the Zn@ SPEEK/30%CD indicates a compact and smooth surface (Fig. S1c). The artificial SPEEK/30%CD interfacial layer has a thickness of about 25 μm in the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image (Fig. 2e), which might develop a hydrophilic/hydrophobic phase separation structure due to the polarity differences between the hydrophobic aromatic backbone of SPEEK, the hydrophilic sulfonic acid groups, and the CDs. The hydrophilic phase (or channels) is formed around the CD nanoparticles (Fig. S1d). After the bare Zn and Zn@SPEEK/30%CD are immersed in 2 M ZnSO4 for 15 days, the bare Zn is overlaid with large pieces of byproduct, which is Zn4(OH)6SO4⋅5H2O (Fig. 2f) [67]. While the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD is only covered by small protrusions (Fig. 2g), indicating a great anti-corrosion ability. Additionally, the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD has a slightly larger contact angle (75.4°) than the bare Zn (72.2°) (Fig. 2h), which is advantageous to prevent the electrolyte from contact with the zinc foil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppressed side reactions and zinc dendrite of Zn@SPEEK/30%CD
ce:section.ce:para: To determine hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) potential, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test is performed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrode exhibits a lower current response and a 136 mV overpotential at 50 mA cm−2 in comparison with bare Zn (−279 mV vs. −143 mV), implying the alleviation of HER in the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD. The Tafel results in Fig. 3b show that the corrosion current of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD is lower than that of the bare Zn and the corrosion potential (−0.951 V) of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD is higher than that of the bare Zn (−0.972 V), denoting the mitigation of the corrosion reaction in the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD. Galvanostatic discharge curves reflect the overpotential for Zn plating (Fig. 3c). The nucleation overpotential (NOP) of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD is lower than that of the bare Zn (22 mV vs. 50 mV). This indicates the superior ion transfer and nucleation propensity on the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD surface, leading to a small nucleus radius and uniform zinc deposition [68,69]. Furthermore, the CV curves of asymmetric cells in Fig. 3d also demonstrate that the NOP of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrode decreases by about 56 mV, further confirming a pronounced reduction in the Zn nucleation barrier. Subsequently, the Zn nucleation and growth behaviors of them are explored via chronoamperometry (CA) test (Fig. S4). The bare Zn displays a random 2D diffusion process according to the continuously increasing current density due to the expanded effective surface area, meaning uneven zinc nucleation and pronounced Zn dendrites. In stark contrast, the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD demonstrates a stable 3D diffusion-dominated process with a suppressed 2D diffusion of less than 30 s at the initial period, verifying the limited and uniform nucleation. Additionally, the zinc ion transference numbers (tZn 2+) are calculated based on the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements before and after the static voltage tests (Fig. S5). The tZn 2+ value of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD increases to 0.76 from the 0.26 of the bare Zn (Fig. 3e), indicating an effectively improved Zn2+ flux and reduced cation concentration gradient. To further uncover the Zn2+ transport kinetics at anode/electrolyte interfaces, the Zn2+ desolvation activation energy (E a) values of the bare Zn (29.13 kJ mol−1) and Zn@SPEEK/30%CD (14.38 kJ mol−1) calculated from the Arrhenius equation are presented in Fig. 3f. Both E a values are respectively close to those in literature (32.2 vs. 14.5 kJ mol−1) [70]. The reduced E a value of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD suggests the enhanced Zn2+ ion transport and accelerated plating/stripping kinetics owing to the suitable zincophilic channels in the artificial SPEEK/30%CD interfacial layer (Fig. 1d).

To gain an insight into the cyclic stability of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrode, symmetric cells are fabricated and examined with Zn@SPEEK/30%CD and bare Zn as electrodes, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the symmetric cell with Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes exhibits consistently lower overpotentials than those of bare Zn electrodes at the current densities ranging from 0.3 to 10 mA cm−2, illuminating the excellent high-rate capability. Moreover, the symmetric cell with Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes reveals stable operation for 800 h at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 without obvious voltage fluctuation (Fig. 4b), signifying superior cycling stability. Conversely, the symmetric cell with bare Zn electrodes only shows a short cycle life of ⁓100 h with large voltage fluctuation. It is comparable to many previously reported Zn anodes with artificial interfacial layers (Table S1), elucidating the advantages of the SPEEK/CD coating for zinc anode protection. The detailed capacity-voltage curves of Fig. 4b also approve the stable polarization and high reversibility of Zn plating/stripping of Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes (Fig. 4c). When the cells are performed at 2 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2, the bare Zn cell experiences short-circuiting within 50 h. While the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD still maintains a significantly extended lifespan of 550 h (Fig. S6).

To investigate the reversibility of the repeated charge-discharge of the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrode, asymmetric cells are measured at 5 mA cm−2 and 2.5 mAh cm−2 (Fig. 4d). The bare Zn||Cu asymmetric cell indicates Coulombic efficiency (CE) with notable fluctuation and a sudden failure within 500 h. Comparatively, the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD||Cu asymmetric cell accomplishes over 1600 plating/stripping cycles with an average CE of approximately 100 %, verifying the suppression of the zinc dendrites and side reactions by the SPEEK/CD coating. Besides, in situ optical microscopy is employed to visually observe the Zn plating behavior. As shown in Fig. 4e, the bare Zn gradually grows massive moss-like dendrites after 60 min at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2, whereas the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD maintains a uniform and stable surface during the entire operation process, indicating the effectively inhibited Zn dendrite growth. The morphological features of the zinc foils after 50 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 in symmetric cells are observed through SEM images. The bare Zn electrode exhibits severe dendrite formation (Fig. 4f). In comparison, the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrode only displays dense and flat Zn deposition (Fig. 4g).

The aforementioned results underscore that the SPEEK/30%CD layer can effectively suppress HER and parasitic reactions, notably decrease the energy barrier for Zn nucleation, remarkably promote even Zn deposition, significantly improve the reversibility of the Zn plating/stripping, and ultimately achieve outstanding cycle performance of Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of ZIHS devices with coated Zn anode
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the merits of the SPEEK/30%CD layer for Zn anodes, an aqueous ZIHS device is assembled by employing AC as the cathode material and Zn@SPEEK/30%CD as the anode to explore the practical application. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the appropriate operating voltage window for the device is 0–1.8 V, which is consistent with the LSV result (Fig. S7). The CV and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves (Fig. 5b and c) of the device exhibit good symmetry, indicating excellent reversibility. At current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 5 A g−1, the ZIHS delivers specific capacitances of 197.1, 169.9, 148.8, 139.1, and 123.2 F g−1, respectively. It holds an energy density of 79.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 369.9 W kg−1. The specific capacitance at 5 A g−1 remains 62.5 % of the initial one at 0.5 A g−1 (Fig. 5d), revealing its excellent rate performance. The Nyquist plot of the device is presented in Fig. 5e. The equivalent series resistance (R s) including the current collector, electrode materials, and electrolyte is as low as 1.17 Ω, hinting a high conductivity. While the charge transfer resistance (R ct) of 9.50 Ω evaluated from the semi-circle at middle frequencies signifies fast ion transfer. In addition, the stability of the device is evaluated by undertaking long-time charge/discharge cycles at 5 A g−1. Noticeably, the device demonstrates a capacity retention of 90.8 % and nearly 100 % CE after 20,000 cycles. Lastly, the device is used to drive a mini-fan to exemplify the practicability (Fig. S8).

In summary, a facile and effective strategy for zinc anode protection has been well developed. The SPEEK/CD interfacial layer constructed on Zn anodes formed a hydrophilic/hydrophobic phase separation structure with broad hydrophilic channels for zinc ions, which not only promoted the migration of zinc ions, but also prevented the transmission of anions and water molecules. Benefiting from the unique structure of the SPEEK/CD coating, the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrode eliminates Zn dendrites due to the homogeneous stripping/plating, mitigates the HER, and reduces the side reactions. Moreover, the Zn||Zn symmetrical cell using the Zn@SPEEK/30%CD electrodes achieves a remarkably long-term performance of over 800 h at 1 mA cm−2. The resulting ZIHS exhibits long cycling durability of 20,000 cycles with nearly 100 % CE. It is expected that this work would provide a convenient strategy to solve the multiple challenges faced by zinc anodes and propel the development of stable and safe zinc-metal energy storage devices.

Xi Cheng: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Jingjing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Jing Xu: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Hongjun Li: Writing – review & editing. Hongwei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Danying Zuo: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the Analytical and Testing Center of Wuhan Textile University for SEM testing.
This work was supported by the open fund of the Hubei Engineering Research Center for Industrial Detonator Intelligent Assembly of Wuhan Textile University (WGK202402001).
